task: We’ve got a storm system moving in, and I’m trying to decide what’s worth doing ahead of time so I’m not scrambling if the lights go out. Can you help me make a practical checklist for the house, food, and anything important to set aside first?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a practical, “do this first” storm prep checklist you can use right away.

## Storm Prep Checklist

### 1) House
- [ ] Charge phones, power banks, laptops, battery lanterns
- [ ] Test flashlights; put extras where you can reach them fast
- [ ] Set fridge/freezer to coldest safe setting
- [ ] Bring in or secure loose outdoor items
- [ ] Close and latch windows, doors, garage
- [ ] Park vehicles where falling branches are less likely
- [ ] Clear drains/gutters if easy and safe
- [ ] Move important papers and valuables to a dry, higher spot
- [ ] Know where the main water shutoff is
- [ ] If you have a generator: check it works, fuel it as needed, and keep it outside only

### 2) Food and water
- [ ] Fill water jugs, pitchers, and clean containers
- [ ] Set aside 3 days of easy food that does not need cooking
- [ ] Use up or move perishables to the front of the fridge
- [ ] Keep a manual can opener with the food
- [ ] Put snacks, pet food, and baby supplies in one easy-to-grab spot

### 3) Important items to set aside first
- [ ] Insurance papers
- [ ] IDs, cash, keys
- [ ] Medications and glasses
- [ ] Phone chargers and battery packs
- [ ] Flashlight
- [ ] Any medical devices or backup supplies
- [ ] Pet leash/carrier and supplies

### 4) Before the storm gets worse
- [ ] Top off car fuel if you may need to leave
- [ ] Put a cooler/ice packs ready if power loss is likely
- [ ] Make sure someone else in the house knows the plan
- [ ] Save important phone numbers on paper

If you want, I can turn this into a tighter **one-page printable checklist** with only the highest-priority items.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty good, but it’s a little broad. I’d want it trimmed down to the stuff that actually matters if the power’s out for a day or two. Also, put the paper records higher on the list — insurance, ID, meds, bank info, house keys. That’s the part people forget until they’re already stressed.

And don’t tell me to just “make sure someone knows the plan.” I’m not looking to depend on anybody. Give me the checklist I can handle myself, with the no-nonsense order of what to do first.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here’s a tighter, no-nonsense **power-outage checklist for 1–2 days**, ordered by what matters most if you’re handling it yourself.

## First: grab the important stuff
- [ ] **ID / wallet**
- [ ] **House keys / car keys**
- [ ] **Insurance papers** or photos of them
- [ ] **Bank info** you may need quickly
- [ ] **Medications**
- [ ] **Glasses / contacts**
- [ ] **Phone**
- [ ] **Chargers / power bank**
- [ ] **Cash**
- [ ] **Any medical device supplies** you rely on

Put all of that in one place you can reach fast if you need to leave.

## Then: make the house ready
- [ ] Charge phone and power bank
- [ ] Charge flashlight or lanterns
- [ ] Set fridge/freezer colder if needed
- [ ] Close windows, doors, and garage
- [ ] Bring in or secure anything loose outside
- [ ] Move paper records and valuables to a dry, high spot
- [ ] Know where the main water shutoff is

## Then: food and water
- [ ] Fill clean containers with water
- [ ] Put out 1–2 days of no-cook food
- [ ] Keep a manual can opener with the food
- [ ] Put snacks where you can reach them in the dark
- [ ] If you have baby or pet supplies, set those aside too

## Then: if the storm is likely to knock power out
- [ ] Top off your gas tank if you may need to drive
- [ ] Put a flashlight by the bed and another in the main living area
- [ ] Set aside a cooler and ice packs if you have them
- [ ] Keep important papers together, not scattered

## What to ignore for now
- Deep cleaning
- Nonessential organizing
- Anything that can wait until power is back

If you want, I can turn this into a **super-short “do this in order” version** with just 10 checkboxes.

user_simulator.text: That’s better. Straight to the point, and the papers are where they ought to be. I’d keep the “bank info” part a little more specific though — account numbers or a printed note, not the whole mess of statements. And I’d add a spare house key if it isn’t already in that first pile. Otherwise this is the kind of order I’d actually use.
